title: Hyponatraemia — Workup
oneLiner: Low serum sodium (<135 mmol/L); common, often iatrogenic, but severe acute drop can cause cerebral oedema and death.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cerebral oedema (acute hyponatraemia <48h):** Rapid brain swelling → herniation. Key finding: declining GCS, seizures, Cushing’s reflex, fixed pupils. **Immediate hypertonic saline needed** (3% NaCl, not normal saline).
- **Hypovolaemic shock with hyponatraemia:** Usually from GI losses (vomiting, diarrhoea) or diuretics. Key finding: hypotension, tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, raised urea. **Do not give normal saline if euvolaemic/hypervolaemic** — but hypovolaemic needs fluid resuscitation first.
- **Adrenal crisis (Addison’s):** Hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + hypoglycaemia. Key finding: shock not responding to fluids, pigmentation, postural hypotension. **Give IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat** before cortisol results back.
sections.Presentation:
- **Asymptomatic** if mild/moderate chronic (>48h): incidental lab finding.
- **Symptomatic** if acute (<48h) or severe (<120 mmol/L): nausea/vomiting, headache, confusion, seizures, coma.
- **Exam:** Euvolemia vs hypovolaemia vs hypervolaemia (jugular venous pressure, oedema, postural BP). Neurological exam (GCS, focal signs, papilloedema).
- **Medication history:** Thiazides, SSRIs, carbamazepine, desmopressin, OCP, NSAIDs.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Hypovolaemic hyponatraemia** (fluid loss > water loss): GI losses, diuretics, salt-wasting nephropathy, adrenal insufficiency.
- **Euvolemic hyponatraemia** (water excess): SIADH (commonest), primary polydipsia, hypothyroidism, glucocorticoid deficiency (not mineralocorticoid).
- **Hypervolaemic hyponatraemia** (water and sodium retention): Heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, renal failure.
- **Splitter:** Check urine sodium and osmolality.
  - Urine Na <20 mmol/L → hypovolaemia or hypervolaemia (kidney retaining Na). Need clinical volume assessment to differentiate.
  - Urine Na >40 mmol/L → euvolemic SIADH or renal salt wasting (or diuretic use). Add urine osmolality: if >100 mOsm/kg then SIADH (inappropriate concentration); if <100 then primary polydipsia or beer potomania.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Capillary glucose (exclude hypoglycaemia in suspected adrenal crisis).
  - Urine dipstick (ketones in DKA, proteinuria in nephrotic syndrome).
  - Postural BP (if tolerated).
  - ECG (check long QT — can occur with severe hypokalaemia, but here focus on hyponatraemia; also rule out arrhythmia).
- **Bloods:**
  - Serum Na, K, urea, creatinine, glucose, albumin.
  - Serum osmolality (calculated or measured — if measured >295 mOsm/kg, think pseudohyponatraemia or hyperglycaemia).
  - Thyroid function (TSH), cortisol (9am or random — not for immediate emergencies).
  - Repeat Na after 4-6h if correcting actively.
  - **Never-miss test:** Urine osmolality and spot urine sodium on first void (before giving IV fluids). This is diagnostic.
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR (if suspected SIADH from lung pathology — small cell, pneumonia, TB).
  - CT head (if focal neurology, severe headache, or no cause found).
- **Special tests:**
  - Short synacthen test if adrenal insufficiency suspected.
  - ACTH/cortisol levels.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **SIADH diagnostic criteria (Bartter-Schwartz):**
    - Hypo-osmolality (serum osmolality <275 mOsm/kg)
    - Inappropriately concentrated urine (>100 mOsm/kg)
    - Elevated urine Na (>40 mmol/L) with normal salt/water intake
    - Exclusion of hypovolaemia, adrenal insufficiency, hypothyroidism, diuretics.
  - **Rate of correction:** Never exceed 8-10 mmol/L in 24h (8-10 mmol/L in 24h, 18 mmol/L in 48h) to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Severe acute symptoms (GCS<13, seizures, coma):** Call senior/ICU. Administer 3% NaCl (hypertonic saline) 100-150 mL bolus over 10 min (can repeat x2 until symptoms improve). Aim for 4-6 mmol/L rise in first few hours **not** full correction.
  - **Hypovolaemic:** 0.9% saline (IV normal saline) 500 mL bolus, reassess. If adrenal crisis, give hydrocortisone 100 mg IV.
  - **Hypervolaemic:** Fluid restriction (1 L/day) + treat underlying cause (diuretics if heart failure, stop water intake).
  - **Euvolemic (probable SIADH):** Fluid restriction (500-800 mL/day) first line. Do not give normal saline — it will worsen hyponatraemia.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Check Na every 4-6h during active correction.
  - **Monitoring for ODS (overcorrection):** If Na corrects >10 mmol/L in 24h, consider therapeutic re-lowering with DDAVP or free water (under expert guidance).
  - **Treat specific causes:** Stop thiazides, replace steroids, treat lung pathology.
  - **SIADH second-line:** Demeclocycline 600-1200 mg/day (slower onset) or Vaptans (tolvaptan) — [check local policy] for use, typically restricted to specialist.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If chronic SIADH: fluid restriction indefinitely + dietary management.
  - Review all medications that may cause hyponatraemia.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** If euvolemic/hypervolaemic → likely restrict to 500-800 mL/day oral fluid. IV fluids only if hypovolaemic or emergency. Do not give hypotonic fluids (e.g. 5% dextrose).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Yes if not hypovolaemic (use LMWH [check local policy]).
  - **Diet/NBM:** Usually normal unless vomiting.
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 hourly if severe, 4-hourly if stable; neuro observations if CGI.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Hypertonic saline (3% NaCl)** — for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia: 100-150 mL IV over 10 min (via central line ideally, but peripheral in emergency). [Check local policy] for bag availability.
- **Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat** — if suspected adrenal crisis.
- **Demeclocycline** — for SIADH if fluid restriction fails: 600 mg BD or 300 mg QDS. Not first-line due to nephrotoxicity.
- **Tolvaptan (vaptan)** — [check local policy] — used in SIADH but risk of overcorrection. Must be initiated by specialist.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Demeclocycline avoid in renal impairment. Tolvaptan restrict to those with normal liver and eGFR.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Overcorrecting hyponatraemia with too-rapid rise (e.g. giving multiple boluses of 3% NaCl without checking Na level). **Never correct faster than 8 mmol/L/24h in chronic hyponatraemia.** If overcorrection occurs, use DDAVP or free water to re-lower Na.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Cerebral oedema, seizures, herniation (acute severe hyponatraemia).
  - Hypovolaemic shock.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS)** — pontine and extrapontine myelinolysis due to rapid correction. Presents with dysarthria, dysphagia, quadriparesis, locked-in syndrome. Permanent.
  - Falls, fractures due to confusion/weakness.
sections.Prognosis:
- Asymptomatic chronic mild hyponatraemia (Na >125) usually resolves with treating cause.
- Severe hyponatraemia (Na <120) with acute onset has high mortality (25-50%) without prompt treatment. ODS has poor prognosis; many survivors have permanent neurological deficits.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do the urine Na/urine osmolality** on the first sample before any fluids — it’s the most important test.
- **Check the patient’s drug chart** for thiazides, SSRIs, carbamazepine, antidepressants — these are common culprits.
- **Use the “Euvolemic vs Hypovolaemic” clinical assessment** (postural BP, JVP, mucus membranes) to guide initial fluid strategy.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Giving normal saline to a euvolemic or hypervolemic patient** — it will not correct SIADH and may worsen water retention.
- **Ignoring adrenal insufficiency** — if you see hyponatraemia with hyperkalaemia and hypoglycaemia, treat hydrocortisone before cortisol results.
- **Correcting too rapidly in chronic hyponatraemia** — aim for 4-6 mmol/L in first 24h, not full normalisation. Check Na after each bolus.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening headache, confusion, vomiting, new focal weakness, seizures, or loss of consciousness.
- **Follow-up:** GP or renal/general medicine clinic in 1-2 weeks to check Na and adjust plan (e.g. fluid restriction adherence, medication review).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - GCS < 13 or seizure.
  - Na < 120 with acute symptoms (cerebral oedema risk).
  - Overcorrection >10 mmol/L in 24h (needs neurology review and possibly desmopressin).
  - Shock not responsive to fluid (consider adrenal crisis).
- **NEWS2 ≥ 7** or lactate > 4 mmol/L (if available).

rawMarkdown:
## Hyponatraemia — Workup
**One-liner:** Low serum sodium (<135 mmol/L); common, often iatrogenic, but severe acute drop can cause cerebral oedema and death.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Cerebral oedema (acute hyponatraemia <48h):** Rapid brain swelling → herniation. Key finding: declining GCS, seizures, Cushing’s reflex, fixed pupils. **Immediate hypertonic saline needed** (3% NaCl, not normal saline).
- **Hypovolaemic shock with hyponatraemia:** Usually from GI losses (vomiting, diarrhoea) or diuretics. Key finding: hypotension, tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, raised urea. **Do not give normal saline if euvolaemic/hypervolaemic** — but hypovolaemic needs fluid resuscitation first.
- **Adrenal crisis (Addison’s):** Hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + hypoglycaemia. Key finding: shock not responding to fluids, pigmentation, postural hypotension. **Give IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat** before cortisol results back.

### Presentation
- **Asymptomatic** if mild/moderate chronic (>48h): incidental lab finding.
- **Symptomatic** if acute (<48h) or severe (<120 mmol/L): nausea/vomiting, headache, confusion, seizures, coma.
- **Exam:** Euvolemia vs hypovolaemia vs hypervolaemia (jugular venous pressure, oedema, postural BP). Neurological exam (GCS, focal signs, papilloedema).
- **Medication history:** Thiazides, SSRIs, carbamazepine, desmopressin, OCP, NSAIDs.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Hypovolaemic hyponatraemia** (fluid loss > water loss): GI losses, diuretics, salt-wasting nephropathy, adrenal insufficiency.
- **Euvolemic hyponatraemia** (water excess): SIADH (commonest), primary polydipsia, hypothyroidism, glucocorticoid deficiency (not mineralocorticoid).
- **Hypervolaemic hyponatraemia** (water and sodium retention): Heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, renal failure.
- **Splitter:** Check urine sodium and osmolality.
  - Urine Na <20 mmol/L → hypovolaemia or hypervolaemia (kidney retaining Na). Need clinical volume assessment to differentiate.
  - Urine Na >40 mmol/L → euvolemic SIADH or renal salt wasting (or diuretic use). Add urine osmolality: if >100 mOsm/kg then SIADH (inappropriate concentration); if <100 then primary polydipsia or beer potomania.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Capillary glucose (exclude hypoglycaemia in suspected adrenal crisis).
  - Urine dipstick (ketones in DKA, proteinuria in nephrotic syndrome).
  - Postural BP (if tolerated).
  - ECG (check long QT — can occur with severe hypokalaemia, but here focus on hyponatraemia; also rule out arrhythmia).
- **Bloods:**
  - Serum Na, K, urea, creatinine, glucose, albumin.
  - Serum osmolality (calculated or measured — if measured >295 mOsm/kg, think pseudohyponatraemia or hyperglycaemia).
  - Thyroid function (TSH), cortisol (9am or random — not for immediate emergencies).
  - Repeat Na after 4-6h if correcting actively.
  - **Never-miss test:** Urine osmolality and spot urine sodium on first void (before giving IV fluids). This is diagnostic.
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR (if suspected SIADH from lung pathology — small cell, pneumonia, TB).
  - CT head (if focal neurology, severe headache, or no cause found).
- **Special tests:**
  - Short synacthen test if adrenal insufficiency suspected.
  - ACTH/cortisol levels.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **SIADH diagnostic criteria (Bartter-Schwartz):**
    - Hypo-osmolality (serum osmolality <275 mOsm/kg)
    - Inappropriately concentrated urine (>100 mOsm/kg)
    - Elevated urine Na (>40 mmol/L) with normal salt/water intake
    - Exclusion of hypovolaemia, adrenal insufficiency, hypothyroidism, diuretics.
  - **Rate of correction:** Never exceed 8-10 mmol/L in 24h (8-10 mmol/L in 24h, 18 mmol/L in 48h) to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Severe acute symptoms (GCS<13, seizures, coma):** Call senior/ICU. Administer 3% NaCl (hypertonic saline) 100-150 mL bolus over 10 min (can repeat x2 until symptoms improve). Aim for 4-6 mmol/L rise in first few hours **not** full correction.
  - **Hypovolaemic:** 0.9% saline (IV normal saline) 500 mL bolus, reassess. If adrenal crisis, give hydrocortisone 100 mg IV.
  - **Hypervolaemic:** Fluid restriction (1 L/day) + treat underlying cause (diuretics if heart failure, stop water intake).
  - **Euvolemic (probable SIADH):** Fluid restriction (500-800 mL/day) first line. Do not give normal saline — it will worsen hyponatraemia.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Check Na every 4-6h during active correction.
  - **Monitoring for ODS (overcorrection):** If Na corrects >10 mmol/L in 24h, consider therapeutic re-lowering with DDAVP or free water (under expert guidance).
  - **Treat specific causes:** Stop thiazides, replace steroids, treat lung pathology.
  - **SIADH second-line:** Demeclocycline 600-1200 mg/day (slower onset) or Vaptans (tolvaptan) — [check local policy] for use, typically restricted to specialist.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If chronic SIADH: fluid restriction indefinitely + dietary management.
  - Review all medications that may cause hyponatraemia.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** If euvolemic/hypervolaemic → likely restrict to 500-800 mL/day oral fluid. IV fluids only if hypovolaemic or emergency. Do not give hypotonic fluids (e.g. 5% dextrose).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Yes if not hypovolaemic (use LMWH [check local policy]).
  - **Diet/NBM:** Usually normal unless vomiting.
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 hourly if severe, 4-hourly if stable; neuro observations if CGI.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Hypertonic saline (3% NaCl)** — for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia: 100-150 mL IV over 10 min (via central line ideally, but peripheral in emergency). [Check local policy] for bag availability.
- **Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat** — if suspected adrenal crisis.
- **Demeclocycline** — for SIADH if fluid restriction fails: 600 mg BD or 300 mg QDS. Not first-line due to nephrotoxicity.
- **Tolvaptan (vaptan)** — [check local policy] — used in SIADH but risk of overcorrection. Must be initiated by specialist.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Demeclocycline avoid in renal impairment. Tolvaptan restrict to those with normal liver and eGFR.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Overcorrecting hyponatraemia with too-rapid rise (e.g. giving multiple boluses of 3% NaCl without checking Na level). **Never correct faster than 8 mmol/L/24h in chronic hyponatraemia.** If overcorrection occurs, use DDAVP or free water to re-lower Na.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Cerebral oedema, seizures, herniation (acute severe hyponatraemia).
  - Hypovolaemic shock.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS)** — pontine and extrapontine myelinolysis due to rapid correction. Presents with dysarthria, dysphagia, quadriparesis, locked-in syndrome. Permanent.
  - Falls, fractures due to confusion/weakness.

### Prognosis
- Asymptomatic chronic mild hyponatraemia (Na >125) usually resolves with treating cause.
- Severe hyponatraemia (Na <120) with acute onset has high mortality (25-50%) without prompt treatment. ODS has poor prognosis; many survivors have permanent neurological deficits.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do the urine Na/urine osmolality** on the first sample before any fluids — it’s the most important test.
- **Check the patient’s drug chart** for thiazides, SSRIs, carbamazepine, antidepressants — these are common culprits.
- **Use the “Euvolemic vs Hypovolaemic” clinical assessment** (postural BP, JVP, mucus membranes) to guide initial fluid strategy.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Giving normal saline to a euvolemic or hypervolemic patient** — it will not correct SIADH and may worsen water retention.
- **Ignoring adrenal insufficiency** — if you see hyponatraemia with hyperkalaemia and hypoglycaemia, treat hydrocortisone before cortisol results.
- **Correcting too rapidly in chronic hyponatraemia** — aim for 4-6 mmol/L in first 24h, not full normalisation. Check Na after each bolus.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening headache, confusion, vomiting, new focal weakness, seizures, or loss of consciousness.
- **Follow-up:** GP or renal/general medicine clinic in 1-2 weeks to check Na and adjust plan (e.g. fluid restriction adherence, medication review).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - GCS < 13 or seizure.
  - Na < 120 with acute symptoms (cerebral oedema risk).
  - Overcorrection >10 mmol/L in 24h (needs neurology review and possibly desmopressin).
  - Shock not responsive to fluid (consider adrenal crisis).
- **NEWS2 ≥ 7** or lactate > 4 mmol/L (if available).
